topicArea: Philosophy of Privacy, the Nothing-to-Hide Retort & the Conditions of Self-Formation

questions.passage:
The retort arrives at every dinner table where the subject comes up. Someone mentions the doorbell cameras, the location histories, the inboxes scanned for advertising cues, and someone else shrugs: let them look, I have nothing to hide. The shrug is not stupid. It rests on a definite theory of what privacy is, namely a screen for discreditable facts, and on that theory the honest have surrendered nothing when the screen comes down. The trouble is that the theory is wrong about its subject, and the shrug, examined, turns out to concede a great deal that its author would not knowingly give away.

Start with what the theory gets right. Privacy does sometimes function as concealment, and concealment does sometimes protect wrongdoing; every fraud has a use for a locked drawer. If that exhausted the concept, the calculus would be simple: privacy would be a tax the innocent pay so the guilty can hide, and reducing it would be a public service. Surveillance, on this accounting, injures only those with something to conceal, and objections to it amount to special pleading on their behalf.

The first thing the accounting misses is that information is not appropriate or inappropriate in itself but relative to context. A medical history disclosed to a physician is care; the same history disclosed to an employer is leverage. A joke made among friends is friendship; verbatim before a hiring committee, it is evidence. What people guard when they guard privacy is mostly not discreditable fact but the boundaries between contexts, each with its own norms of flow, and the characteristic injury of surveillance is not exposure of the guilty but the collapse of those boundaries, so that words and acts calibrated for one audience are conscripted to answer before another they never addressed.

The second thing missed is what unobserved space is for. A self is not found complete; it is drafted, and drafts are unpresentable by nature. The unformed opinion tried aloud, the identity worn for a season, the book read out of curiosity one would not defend at a meeting, these are the workshop of a person, and the workshop closes when a permanent audience is installed. The observed self performs, and knows it performs; anticipation does the disciplining long before any file is used, which is why the deepest cost of surveillance appears nowhere in its ledgers. It is the sentence unwritten, the search not run, the meeting not attended, a subtraction from the drafts of the self that no one, including the subject, can ever fully audit.

Third, the shrug treats privacy as a private possession, mine to keep or spend, when the good in question is stubbornly collective. My address book discloses your affiliations; my genome carries my siblings' futures; my innocuous fragments, pooled with millions of others, train the instruments that sort us all into categories none of us chose, with consequences for people who consented to nothing. Where inference runs on aggregates, the individual waiver is a fiction of scale: one person's open door ventilates the whole street, and the language of personal choice systematically miscounts the stakes.

None of this makes privacy an unconditional good, and the concept's defenders weaken their case when they pretend otherwise. Closed doors have sheltered cruelty as reliably as intimacy; institutions and officials invoke confidentiality to escape scrutiny that is properly theirs to bear; and a slogan like privacy for the weak, transparency for the powerful, whatever its appeal, only relocates the argument, since weakness and power are matters of degree and position, and most people are each in some relation. The honest conclusion is that privacy is a structural good with abusers, like property or speech, to be shaped rather than maximized or dismissed.

What the dinner-table shrug finally concedes, then, is not the contents of a drawer but a set of terms: that contexts may be collapsed at will, that the workshop of the self may be permanently attended, that goods held jointly may be waived severally, and that the ledger of who knows what about whom may be kept by whoever finds the keeping profitable. Nothing to hide was never the question. The question is who holds the record, what the holding licenses, and what kind of persons are formed in a world where the drafts are never private, and it deserves better than a shrug, because the answer arrives whether or not it is given.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because closed doors have sheltered cruelty and officials abuse confidentiality, privacy should be curtailed for citizens and institutions alike.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surveillance injures only those with discreditable facts to conceal, so honest people lose nothing when observation expands.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nothing-to-hide retort rests on a mistaken theory of privacy as concealment of wrongdoing, missing privacy's real work in maintaining contextual boundaries, protecting the unobserved space where selves are formed, and securing a collective good that individual waivers cannot manage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Privacy is an unconditional individual right that no institutional interest can legitimately outweigh.

explanations.A: This choice converts the sixth paragraph's honest concession into the essay's conclusion. The passage acknowledges privacy's abusers in order to argue that privacy is a structural good to be shaped rather than maximized or dismissed; curtailment across the board is precisely neither of those verdicts.
explanations.B: This choice restates the theory the passage sets out to dismantle. The second paragraph presents this accounting only to identify what it misses: contextual boundaries, the workshop of the self, and the collective character of informational goods, each developed in a subsequent paragraph as a refutation.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage diagnoses the retort's underlying theory, privacy as a screen for discreditable facts, and replaces it with three arguments: information's appropriateness is contextual and surveillance collapses contexts, unobserved space is where selves are drafted and anticipation disciplines before any file is used, and privacy is collective, so individual waivers miscount stakes, concluding that the real question concerns who holds the record and what the holding licenses.
explanations.D: This choice asserts the absolutism the sixth paragraph explicitly disclaims. The passage states that privacy is not an unconditional good, that closed doors have sheltered cruelty, and that defenders weaken their case by pretending otherwise, so no unconditional right is being claimed.

questions.stem: The primary function of the sixth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: qualify the passage's defense of privacy by conceding its abuses and rejecting both maximization and slogans, thereby positioning privacy as a structural good requiring shaping.

choices.label: B
choices.text: retract the earlier arguments about contextual boundaries and self-formation as overstated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: endorse the principle of privacy for the weak and transparency for the powerful as the passage's final position.

choices.label: D
choices.text: demonstrate that institutional confidentiality is always illegitimate.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph opens by denying that privacy is an unconditional good, concedes that closed doors shelter cruelty and that officials abuse confidentiality, examines and sets aside the weak-versus-powerful slogan because power is a matter of degree and position, and lands on the passage's calibrated stance: privacy as a structural good with abusers, like property or speech, to be shaped rather than maximized or dismissed.
explanations.B: This choice mistakes qualification for retraction. The contextual and self-formation arguments stand untouched; the paragraph limits the conclusion drawn from them, warning defenders against absolutism, and the final paragraph reasserts those arguments in summary form.
explanations.C: This choice adopts a slogan the paragraph explicitly declines. The passage grants its appeal but says it only relocates the argument, since weakness and power are matters of degree and most people occupy both positions in different relations.
explanations.D: This choice inflates one concession into a universal verdict. The paragraph observes that confidentiality is invoked to escape scrutiny that is properly borne, an abuse claim about some invocations, not a ruling that institutional confidentiality is illegitimate as such.

questions.stem: The phrase the drafts of the self, as used in the fourth paragraph, most nearly refers to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the official records that institutions compile about individuals over time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: written diaries in which people record their private reflections.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the performances people stage once they know they are being observed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the unformed opinions, provisional identities, and exploratory acts through which a person is gradually worked out, which require unobserved space precisely because they are unpresentable by nature.

explanations.A: This choice locates the drafts in institutional files, reversing the paragraph's geometry. The drafts belong to the person's own formation; the files belong to observers, and the paragraph's point is that permanent observation subtracts from the former in ways no file records.
explanations.B: This choice narrows the metaphor to one literal genre of writing. Diaries could be among the workshop's contents, but the paragraph's examples, opinions tried aloud, identities worn for a season, curious reading, show the drafts to be the whole exploratory activity of self-formation, not a document type.
explanations.C: This choice names the opposite of the drafts. The observed self performs, and knows it performs, is the paragraph's description of what replaces drafting when the workshop closes; performance is what surveillance produces, while the drafts are what it forecloses.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph states that a self is drafted rather than found complete, that drafts are unpresentable by nature, and gives the workshop's inventory: the unformed opinion tried aloud, the identity worn for a season, the book read out of curiosity, all of which require space without a permanent audience, since anticipation disciplines before any file is used.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the person who offers the nothing-to-hide retort is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: contemptuous, treating the retort as beneath serious engagement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: respectful of the retort's coherence while arguing that its underlying theory misidentifies what privacy is and concedes more than its author realizes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: persuaded, since the passage concludes that honest people genuinely lose nothing under surveillance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: indifferent, since the passage treats the dispute as a matter of taste on which nothing turns.

explanations.A: This choice misses the passage's explicit courtesy. The shrug is called not stupid and credited with resting on a definite theory whose partial truth the second paragraph states fairly; the engagement is sustained across the whole essay, which is the opposite of dismissal.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage grants that the retort rests on a definite theory and that concealment sometimes protects wrongdoing, then argues the theory is wrong about its subject, developing the contextual, formative, and collective accounts of privacy, and concludes that the shrug concedes terms, about contexts, the workshop of the self, and who keeps the ledger, that its author would not knowingly give away.
explanations.C: This choice attributes to the author the position the passage exists to refute. The claim that surveillance injures only the guilty is presented as the accounting whose omissions the third, fourth, and fifth paragraphs then supply.
explanations.D: This choice contradicts the passage's closing urgency. The final paragraph insists the question deserves better than a shrug because the answer arrives whether or not it is given, treating the stakes as real and formative, not as a matter of taste.

questions.stem: An employer offers employees a discount on insurance premiums if they share data from their fitness trackers, emphasizing that participation is voluntary and individual. Based on the fifth paragraph, the author would most likely object that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: fitness data is inherently discreditable and should never be disclosed to anyone.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the employer has violated the norms of the medical context by requesting the data directly rather than through physicians.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the discount is too small to compensate employees fairly for the market value of their data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the individual, voluntary framing miscounts the stakes, since pooled data trains instruments that sort participants and non-participants alike, making one person's waiver consequential for others who consented to nothing.

explanations.A: This choice invokes the concealment theory of privacy the passage rejects. Nothing in the argument treats fitness data as discreditable; the objection concerns aggregation and collective consequence, not the shamefulness of any fact.
explanations.B: This choice borrows the third paragraph's contextual analysis but misapplies it as a procedural rule about physicians. The fifth paragraph's distinct point, which the question targets, is about aggregate inference, not about which professional channel should carry the data.
explanations.C: This choice frames the problem as underpayment, converting the objection into a bargaining matter. The fifth paragraph's argument is that individual waivers are a fiction of scale regardless of compensation, because the harms run through inference on aggregates and land on people outside the transaction.
explanations.D: Correct. The fifth paragraph argues that privacy is stubbornly collective: innocuous fragments pooled across many people train the instruments that sort everyone into unchosen categories, so where inference runs on aggregates, the individual waiver miscounts the stakes, one open door ventilating the whole street, exactly the structure of the voluntary tracker program.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the third paragraph's claim that the characteristic injury of surveillance is the collapse of contextual boundaries rather than the exposure of wrongdoing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that most people cannot name the data brokers that hold files about them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that surveillance systems frequently contain inaccurate records.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Studies finding that the harms people report from data exposure overwhelmingly involve truthful, innocent information reaching audiences it was never calibrated for, such as workplace consequences from private remarks, with cases involving concealed wrongdoing forming a small minority.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that public support for surveillance rises after highly publicized crimes.

explanations.A: This choice documents public ignorance of the data ecosystem, which may be troubling but does not identify what kind of injury exposure inflicts. The claim at issue distinguishes boundary collapse from wrongdoing exposure, and unfamiliarity with brokers speaks to neither.
explanations.B: This choice raises accuracy as the problem, but the third paragraph's claim concerns truthful information moving between contexts. Inaccurate records support a different critique of surveillance, one the passage does not make, and leave the boundary-collapse thesis untested.
explanations.C: Correct. The claim predicts that surveillance's typical harm is words and acts calibrated for one audience being conscripted before another, not the unmasking of the guilty. Studies showing reported harms overwhelmingly involve truthful, innocent information reaching uncalibrated audiences, with concealed wrongdoing a small minority, match the prediction directly and against its rival.
explanations.D: This choice tracks public opinion about surveillance after crimes, which measures political response rather than the character of the injuries surveillance inflicts, and so cannot arbitrate between the concealment and boundary-collapse accounts.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the fourth paragraph's claim that anticipated observation disciplines behavior and subtracts from self-formation before any record is used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that people frequently forget that recording devices are present in their homes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Large longitudinal studies finding that people's exploratory behavior, unconventional reading, tentative expression of unformed views, experimentation with self-presentation, remained statistically unchanged after they learned their environments were comprehensively monitored, across cultures and levels of monitoring salience.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that some writers report producing their best work under deadline pressure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that surveillance records are rarely reviewed by human analysts.

explanations.A: This choice suggests awareness of observation fades, which complicates but does not refute the claim; the paragraph's mechanism operates through anticipation when observation is salient, and intermittent forgetting is compatible with disciplined behavior returning whenever awareness does.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph's claim is behavioral: the observed self performs, and anticipation forecloses the drafts, the search not run, the opinion not tried. Longitudinal evidence that exploratory behavior remains statistically unchanged after people learn of comprehensive monitoring, across cultures and salience levels, would directly contradict the asserted chilling mechanism at its point of application.
explanations.C: This choice concerns productivity under time pressure, a different kind of constraint from observation. Deadlines and audiences are not the same discipline, and writers thriving under the former says nothing about whether anticipated watching forecloses exploratory self-formation.
explanations.D: This choice, far from weakening the claim, is anticipated by it. The paragraph states that anticipation does the disciplining long before any file is used, so infrequent human review is precisely consistent with the mechanism, which operates through the subject's expectation rather than the record's use.
